subject: Computer Science
content: Today we are going to talk about arrays in computer science. An array is one of the most basic and important data structures you will encounter. Think of it as a row of boxes lined up next to each other, where each box can hold one piece of information. Arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, meaning each box is right next to the other in the computers memory. You access any element using its index, which starts at zero. So the first element is at position zero, the second at position one, and so on. This zero-based indexing is standard across most programming languages. Because elements are stored side by side, the computer can find any element instantly using a simple formula: base address plus index times element size. This gives arrays what we call O(1) or constant time access. It does not matter if your array has ten elements or ten million, finding any single element takes the same amount of time. A practical example is a music playlist on your phone. Each song is stored in order, and when you tap song number five, your phone jumps directly to that position without scanning through songs one through four. Arrays work the same way. However, arrays have a fixed size. Once you create an array with ten slots, you cannot easily add an eleventh without creating a new, larger array and copying everything over. This is the main trade-off: fast access but inflexible size. In summary, arrays are ordered, fixed-size collections with instant access to any element. They are the foundation for more complex data structures like stacks, queues, and hash tables that you will learn about later.
summary: Arrays are fixed-size data structures that store elements in contiguous memory, providing O(1) access time. They are foundational to computer science and used in everything from playlists to complex data structures.
